stem: What is the trace of the matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a11]] & [[a12]] & [[a13]] \\ [[a21]] & [[a22]] & [[a23]] \\ [[a31]] & [[a32]] & [[a33]] \end{bmatrix} \)?
solution: The trace is the sum of the main-diagonal entries: \( ([[a11]]) + ([[a22]]) + ([[a33]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This sums every entry of the matrix, not just the main diagonal.
This sums the anti-diagonal instead of the main diagonal.
This multiplies the diagonal entries rather than adding them.
This is off by one from the correct trace.
This is off by one from the correct trace.
This is off by two from the correct trace.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a11]] & [[a12]] \\ [[a21]] & [[a22]] \end{bmatrix} \) and \( B = \begin{bmatrix} [[b11]] & [[b12]] \\ [[b21]] & [[b22]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is \( \operatorname{tr}([[p]]A + [[q]]B) \)?
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the two scaled traces instead of adding them.
This forgets to scale each trace by its own coefficient before adding.
This uses only \( \operatorname{tr}(A) \) scaled by \( p \), forgetting the \( qB \) term entirely.
This is off by one from the correct trace.
This is off by one from the correct trace.
This is off by two from the correct trace.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a11]] & [[a12]] \\ [[a21]] & [[a22]] \end{bmatrix} \) and \( B = \begin{bmatrix} [[b11]] & [[b12]] \\ [[b21]] & [[b22]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is \( \operatorname{tr}(AB) \)?
solution: The trace of a product satisfies \( \operatorname{tr}(AB) = \sum_{i,j} A_{ij}B_{ji} \), so \( \operatorname{tr}(AB) = [[a11]]\cdot[[b11]] + [[a12]]\cdot[[b21]] + [[a21]]\cdot[[b12]] + [[a22]]\cdot[[b22]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \operatorname{tr}(A)\cdot\operatorname{tr}(B) \), which does not equal \( \operatorname{tr}(AB) \) in general.
This only multiplies matching diagonal entries, omitting the off-diagonal cross terms.
This swaps which off-diagonal entries get multiplied together.
This is off by one from the correct trace.
This is off by one from the correct trace.
This is off by two from the correct trace.

stem: The upper-triangular matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a11]] & [[a12]] & [[a13]] \\ 0 & [[a22]] & [[a23]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[a33]] \end{bmatrix} \) has eigenvalues equal to its diagonal entries. What is the sum of the eigenvalues of \( A \)?
solution: For any square matrix, the sum of the eigenvalues always equals the trace. Since \( A \) is triangular, its eigenvalues are exactly its diagonal entries \( [[a11]], [[a22]], [[a33]] \), and their sum is \( \operatorname{tr}(A) = [[a11]]+[[a22]]+[[a33]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the PRODUCT of the eigenvalues (the determinant), not their sum.
This sums the off-diagonal entries instead of the diagonal (eigenvalue) entries.
This omits the third eigenvalue \( a_{33} \) from the sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by two from the correct sum.

solution: Multiplying out, \( A^2 \) has diagonal entries \( [[a11]]^2+[[a12]]\cdot[[a21]] \) and \( [[a21]]\cdot[[a12]]+[[a22]]^2 \), so \( \operatorname{tr}(A^2) = [[a11]]^2 + [[a22]]^2 + 2\cdot[[a12]]\cdot[[a21]] = [[answer]] \). (Note \( \operatorname{tr}(A^2) \ne \operatorname{tr}(A)^2 \) in general.)
distractor_rationales: This squares the TRACE of \( A \) first, which does not equal \( \operatorname{tr}(A^2) \) in general.
This omits the cross term \( 2a_{12}a_{21} \) that comes from matrix multiplication.
This doubles the trace of \( A \) instead of computing the trace of \( A^2 \).
This is off by one from the correct trace.
This is off by one from the correct trace.
This is off by two from the correct trace.
